content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 14, 2005

Michael R.W. Houston
Rutan & Tucker, LLP
Post Office Box 1950
Costa Mesa, CA 92628-1950

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-248

Dear Mr. Houston:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Orange County Water District ("district") Boardmember Wes Bannister for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Boardmember Bannister participate in a decision regarding whether the district should participate in the California Public Employees Retirement System?

CONCLUSION

Boardmember Bannister has no economic interest which would disqualify him from participating in the CalPERS decision you have identified.

FACTS

Presently, the district maintains a privately managed "defined contribution plan." A defined contribution plan is a benefit plan under which participants make a specific contribution each month (i.e., a "defined contribution") and is maintained in an account for the benefit of these participants. At retirement, the participant has a reservoir of funds from which to draw, based on the sum of the defined contributions made by the participant, plus interest or investment income accruing during the life of the account. In addition to the district's employees, the district's board members may also participate in

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-248
Page No. 2

this program. Under the present pension plan, district board members contribute 7.65%
of their compensation, and the district contributes an equal sum.

The district's board of directors is considering changing from the present
privately run pension plan to the California Public Employees Retirement System
("CalPERS"). This requires that the district become a "contracting agency" with the
CalPERS board of administration.

Boardmember Bannister served on the Huntington Beach City Council from 1986
to 1990. During his three years of service on the city council, the City of Huntington
Beach participated in CalPERS.

Under state law, an employee or other participant (for instance, a city council
member) does not become vested and entitled to CalPERS retirement benefits until he or
she has reached 55 years of age and is credited with five years of service. Even though
Boardmember Bannister is not yet vested in CalPERS, he still maintains a CalPERS
account that accrues interest. According to CalPERS, Bannister's three years of service
on the Huntington Beach City Council counts toward CalPERS's five-year vesting
requirement. Independent of credit for the contributions Bannister made into the
district's current retirement plan, the district's proposed participation in CalPERS would
allow Boardmember Bannister to begin making further contributions into CalPERS to
reach the five-year vesting requirement.

Since 1991, Boardmember Bannister has continuously served as an elected
member of the district's board of directors, amounting to approximately 13 years of
service. If the board of directors elects to participate in CalPERS, Boardmember
Bannister would be credited with his nearly 13 years of service as a district board
member (in addition to the 3 years accrued from his service as a Huntington Beach City
Council member) and would be vested under CalPERS and able to draw pension benefits
upon retirement. If the district's board of directors determines not to participate in
CalPERS, Boardmember Bannister would be able to draw benefits upon retirement from
the district's privately run defined contribution plan, but would not be eligible for
CalPERS benefits.

ANALYSIS

A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
The Commission has adopted a standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a
disqualifying conflict of interest.² (Regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8).)

We have provided an analysis of only steps 1 through 3 because the facts you
have provided do not indicate that Boardmember Bannister will have an economic
interest involved in the CalPERS decision before the board.

_________________________
2 See enclosed Commission publication entitled, "Can I Vote? Conflict of Interest Overview."

File No. A-04-248
Page No. 3

Step 1 – Public Official.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions only apply to public officials. (Sections
87100, 87103; regulation 18700(b)(1).) "Public official" is defined as "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency …" (Section
82048.) As a member of the board of directors of the Orange County Water District,
Wes Bannister is a public official under the Act, and the conflict-of-interest provisions
apply to him.

Step 2 – Making, participating in making, or using official position to influence
governmental decisions.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions only apply to public officials who
"make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b)(2).)

A public official makes a governmental decision when the official, while acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints a person,
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency, or determines not to act. (Regulation
18702.1(a).) A public official participates in making a governmental decision when he or
she, without significant substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes
recommendations regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) Boardmember Bannister
will make a governmental decision if he votes on the CalPERS decision. Additionally, if
Boardmember Bannister engages in any of the actions described above with regard to this
decision, he will "participate in making" or "influence" the decision.

Step 3 – Identifying the economic interests.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions only apply to conflicts of interest arising
out of economic interests as defined in regulations 18703 - 18703.5. A public official has
an economic interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the official, a member of his or her immediate family,
or on any of the following:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation 18703.1(b));

• A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which he or
she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2);

File No. A-04-248
Page No. 4

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregate $500 or more within 12 months prior to
the decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts aggregate $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances
including those of his or her immediate family – this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

Ordinarily, a public official has an economic interest in his or her income, as well
as the income of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)
However, where the employer is a government agency there is typically no economic
interest because of the "government salary exception." (Section 82030(b)(2).) The Act's
definition of "income" expressly excludes "salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
diem received from a... local... government agency..." (Ibid.) The exception also covers
employment-related benefits such as pensions. (In re Moore (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 33.)

Please note that, as discussed above, Boardmember Bannister may have an
economic interest under the "personal financial effects" rule. However, because the
exception of regulation 18705.5(b) applies, we do not further analyze this economic
interest. Regulation 18705.5(b) provides:

"(b) The financial effects of a decision which affects only
the salary, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses the
public official or a member of his or her immediate family
receives from a federal, state, or local government agency
shall not be deemed material, unless the decision is to hire,
fire, promote, demote, suspend without pay or otherwise
take disciplinary action with financial sanction against the
official or a member of his or her immediate family, or to
set a salary for the official or a member of his or her
immediate family which is different from salaries paid to
other employees of the government agency in the same job
classification or position."

File No. A-04-248
Page No. 5


Because Boardmember Bannister's income and benefits are not included in the
definition of income and because regulation 18705.5(b) applies, he has no economic
interest which would disqualify him from participating in the CalPERS decision you have
identified.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [signature] Natalie Bocanegra
Natalie Bocanegra
Counsel, Legal Division

from participating in the CaIPERS decision you have identified.
